banner: Week 12 · Unit 01 Capstone — Build a Polygon You Can Prove
essential_question: Can you build a polygon and then prove, with your own numbers, that it's right?
objective: Design and construct — by compass-and-straightedge or CAD — a polygon of your choice, then verify its perimeter by measurement and its interior angle sum by both measuring and calculating.

agenda.title: Brief — Today You Build & Prove a Polygon

content.heading: Unit 01 skills, one build
bullets: Perimeter (L8) and area formulas (L9) — you'll only need perimeter today.
Classifying triangles + the triangle inequality (L10) — if you build a triangle, your side lengths must obey it.
Angle relationships (L3, L4, L6) — every interior angle you measure should make sense against what you already know.

The test: choose a polygon (triangle, quadrilateral, or pentagon+), construct it with a compass & straightedge (or CAD/drawing tool), then verify its perimeter AND its interior angle sum by measurement.

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Choose a 3-, 4-, or 5-sided polygon. If it's a triangle, pick side lengths that satisfy the triangle inequality before you build. Sketch a plan with every vertex labeled before you construct.

content.heading: Make it real
bullets: Compass & straightedge: step off equal side lengths, swing arcs to find vertices.
Or CAD/drawing tool: draw with exact side lengths and angles, then print or export.
Label every vertex (A, B, C, …) and keep lines clean enough to measure later.

content.preview: Session 3: final mixed review, then MUS Unit Test I on your own.

success_criteria.text: I can construct a polygon with a compass/straightedge (or CAD tool) and label its vertices
I can measure and sum a polygon's side lengths to verify its perimeter
I can measure a polygon's interior angles, sum them, and check that sum against the (n−2)·180° formula

questions.prompt: Measure each side of your polygon and record the lengths, then add them for the perimeter.
questions.answer: Any consistent set of measured side lengths, with units, that sum to the reported perimeter.
questions.explain: Perimeter is just the sum of every side length — units must match.

questions.prompt: Measure each interior angle with a protractor and record all of them.
questions.answer: Angle measures each between 0° and 180° whose sum is close to the theoretical total.
questions.explain: Every interior angle should read between 0° and 180°; small measurement error (a few degrees) is normal.

questions.prompt: Your polygon has n sides. Calculate the theoretical interior angle sum using (n−2)·180°, then compare it to your measured sum. How close were you?
questions.answer: Example: a pentagon (n=5) gives (5−2)·180° = 540°. Compare to your measured total and note the gap.
questions.explain: A small gap (a handful of degrees) is expected from real-world measuring; a huge gap points to a construction or measuring error.

questions.prompt: Reflect: what was harder to make honest — the perimeter (lengths) or the angle sum (angles) — and why?
